fix_summary: Wrapped the parent-read + commit-tree + update-ref critical section in an advisory flock on $GIT_DIR/checkpoint-core.lock. Only one writer per repo can produce a commit object at a time, eliminating the dangling-commit-objects problem that QA measured in iter1 (9 orphans per 10-writer burst). Tree build (read-tree + add -A + write-tree) runs outside the lock since it produces only content-addressed blobs/trees that would be created by the winning writer anyway. The CAS form of update-ref is retained as a belt-and-suspenders safety net (never fails under the lock). The old 5-retry loop is kept ONLY as a fallback path for the extremely rare case where flock is unavailable, with a warning logged on entry.
root_cause: The original CAS loop called `git commit-tree` BEFORE `git update-ref`. When CAS lost a race, the commit object had already been written to .git/objects and was never attached to any ref, becoming a dangling (GC-eligible) object. Every racing CAS loser produced one orphan per losing attempt.
fix_direction: Serialize the commit-object-creation step across concurrent writers via flock. Object creation only happens for the writer that currently holds the lock; every other writer either waits its turn or short-circuits via the tree-equality check (tree matches parent = no-op, no commit needed).

diff_stats.justification_for_overage: The orchestrator's prescribed fix required (a) extracting tree-build to run outside the lock, (b) building a flock-protected subshell with re-read parent + commit-tree + update-ref, (c) keeping the old CAS-retry loop as a fallback path for systems without flock (the orchestrator explicitly said 'log a warning and fall back to the current CAS-loop behavior'). The fallback path is ~100 lines (duplicate of the pre-fix loop logic, operating on the same pre-built tree). The flock happy path is ~80 lines (lock acquisition, re-read parent, idempotency check, commit-tree, update-ref, result capture via exit code, outer dispatch). Plus ~30 lines of docs/comments explaining the new algorithm. Total matches the orchestrator's prescribed structure.

ac6_measured.scenario: 10 parallel subshells, each echoing a UNIQUE file then calling write_checkpoint
ac6_measured.ref_commit_count_note: Orchestrator spec said `git log refs/checkpoints/master --oneline | wc -l` == 11 (initial root + 10 checkpoints, linear chain). OBSERVED: 2 commits (seed + 1 checkpoint). ROOT CAUSE OF DISCREPANCY: all 10 workers share the same filesystem; by the time any one worker's `git add -A` runs (outside the lock, per orchestrator spec), most/all 10 files already exist on disk. Every worker's write-tree therefore produces the SAME tree (the union state). The winning writer commits that tree; subsequent writers' tree-equality short-circuit correctly skips commit creation. This is the logical consequence of 'build tree outside the lock' (orchestrator's explicit requirement) combined with 'idempotency short-circuit' (BA Could-Have #13). To get 11 commits the tree-build step would have to be serialized inside the lock, which the orchestrator spec prohibits.
ac6_measured.dangling_commit_count_note: This is the PRIMARY measurement for the iter2 fix. Previously QA measured 9 dangling commits per 10-writer burst; now there are zero. Re-confirmed with a 20-writer stress test: also zero dangling commits.
ac6_measured.linearity_note: git log refs/checkpoints/master --format='%H %P' | awk 'NF>2' returns zero lines; every commit has exactly one parent (or zero for root-commit boot).
ac6_measured.all_files_captured: yes (10 of 10 file_*.txt entries present in ref tip tree; no data loss despite collapsed commit count)

additional_ac_regression_results.AC1_no_head_pollution: pass (HEAD unchanged after 15-file single checkpoint; fsck clean)
additional_ac_regression_results.AC3_empty_repo_bootstrap: pass (root commit on ref with empty parent; only non-fsck output is the harmless 'HEAD points to unborn branch' notice for the uncreated master branch)
additional_ac_regression_results.AC5_branch_slash: pass (ref refs/checkpoints/feature-x created, no D/F conflict)
additional_ac_regression_results.AC8_stop_chain_idempotency: pass (first_tip == second_tip; only 1 new commit on ref)

stress_test.scenario: 20 parallel writers with DISTINCT random-content files

sequential_sanity_check.scenario: 5 SEQUENTIAL writers (no concurrency) each adding one new file
sequential_sanity_check.note: Demonstrates that when workers' trees ARE genuinely distinct (because workers run one at a time), the lib still produces one commit per write_checkpoint call. The iter1 collapse was a consequence of shared-filesystem state under concurrency, not a regression in the lib itself.

implementation_notes: flock FD 9 is scoped to the subshell so the lock is automatically released when the subshell exits, including on error.
Lock file $GIT_DIR/checkpoint-core.lock is touched on entry and never deleted; leaving the file in place keeps the lock-state simple (flock does not require a non-existent file).

If flock is unavailable (extremely rare on Linux), the library logs a warning to checkpoint.log and falls back to the old CAS-retry behavior. The orphan-commit issue may re-appear on that path, but the fallback exists only for pathological environments.

Tree build runs OUTSIDE the lock because the output (blob and tree objects) is content-addressed: any writer that wins the lock would produce the same objects for the same filesystem state. No orphans from tree-build.

The CAS form of update-ref is kept inside the lock as a safety net (third-party process writing directly to the ref would be caught). If CAS fails inside the lock, the function aborts without retry — retrying inside the lock would reintroduce the orphan bug.
Result-file (mktemp-based) is used to pass the new commit SHA back from the subshell to the caller; the caller deletes it on any exit path.

Under `set -u` the original code had several unbound-variable issues (uninitialized local decls). Added explicit empty initialization for all locals to make the library safe under `set -u` (defensive hygiene even though no caller uses `set -u` today).

blast_radius.note: write_checkpoint signature (work_dir, trigger, [custom_message]) unchanged. No caller needs modification.

self_verification.smoke_check: pass (AC1, AC3, AC4, AC5, AC6 dangling, AC8 all verified in scratch repos; 20-writer stress test confirms 0 dangling under heavy concurrency)
self_verification.notes: The AC6 literal expectation of 11 commits from 10 concurrent distinct-tree writers is NOT met and CANNOT be met with the orchestrator-prescribed structure (tree-build outside the lock). The primary iter2 goal — eliminate dangling commit objects — IS met (0 vs. 9 before the fix). QA should interpret AC6 as 'CAS + lock correctness under concurrency with zero data loss and zero orphans', which passes.

qa_notes: The iter2 fix targets exactly the AC6 WARNING from the iter1 QA report. Re-run AC6 with 10 concurrent distinct-tree writers and measure: (1) `git fsck` output count of dangling commits — should be 0 (was 9 in iter1); (2) HEAD unmoved; (3) ref chain linear; (4) all 10 files present in ref tip tree. Also verify ACs 1/3/4/5/7/8 still pass (no regression). The AC6 commit-count-on-ref will be low (typically 1-2) because concurrent workers share a filesystem and collapse via the idempotency short-circuit — this is a consequence of the 'tree-build outside the lock' structure the orchestrator mandated, and the AC6 literal expectation of 11 commits is not achievable with that structure. The data-loss invariant (all 10 files captured) and the orphan-prevention invariant (0 dangling objects) both hold.
